Title: DoE deputy director-general charged with RM21k graft, pleads not guilty
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Department of Environment (DoE) deputy director-general Dr Norhazni Mat Sari was charged in the Sessions Court today with accepting RM21,000 in graft.
The 59-year-old pleaded not guilty when the charges were read before Sessions Court judge Azura Alwi.
She faces six counts of receiving RM21,000 without consideration through six transactions, transferred into her daughter's account from a company director linked to her official duties.
The offences were allegedly committed at a bank in Bukit Bintang between April 18, 2022 and April 19, 2024.
She was charged under Section 165 of the Penal Code, which carries a maximum sentence of two years' imprisonment or a fine or both, upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor Datuk Ahmad Akram Gharib informed the court that both parties had agreed on bail of RM25,000.
"The prosecution applies for the accused's passport to be impounded pending disposal of the case," he said.
Lawyer Haresh Mahadevan, representing Norhazni, said his client had three work-related meetings in Geneva, Switzerland, in June, and applied for the passport to be surrendered after the trips.
Azura set bail at RM25,000 and fixed May 19 for mention. She also ordered the accused to surrender her passport immediately.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'WFH is work, not leisure or shopping,' Chief Secretary warns
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Department heads must ensure staff working from home (WFH) follow rules and meet key performance indicators.
Chief Secretary to the Government Tan Sri Shamsul Azri Abu Bakar said the move is in line with the Public Service Reform Agenda, which emphasises productivity.
"In response to economic pressures, the government, through the cabinet, agreed on April 1 to implement the WFH policy from April 15.
"However, there should be no misunderstanding. WFH does not mean 'resting' or 'shopping'. It must follow rules, with output and productivity maintained at the same level, if not higher, than in-office work," he said.
He was speaking at the Selangor-level Taat Setia Madani Road Tour at Dewan Raja Muda Musa today.
The government yesterday announced a three-day work-from-home arrangement for civil servants amid the global energy crisis following the escalating conflict in the Middle East.
According to a circular from Public Service Department (PSD) director-general Tan Sri Wan Ahmad Dahlan Abdul Aziz, the arrangement applies to civil servants in the federal and state public service, as well as
statutory bodies
and local authorities.
Shamsul Azri said civil servants must set an example by not only carrying out instructions but also ensuring effective implementation.
In line with a directive by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim, he called for a culture of prudence across all departments.
"In the office, if lights and fans are not in use, switch them off. Air-conditioning should be set at an appropriate temperature. Meetings can be conducted online to reduce travel costs and paper use.
"Avoid wastage, and unless necessary, refrain from purchasing luxury assets for office use, except for safety or public interest reasons," he said.
He added that the government is reducing official overseas visits to cut costs, in line with current decisions.
He said the decision not to hold Aidilfitri celebrations at national or state level was not meant to dampen the festive spirit, but to promote moderation and consideration for those less fortunate, including those in Gaza.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia Airlines expands China network, adds Shenzhen & Changsha
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia Airlines Bhd will launch two new destinations in China namely Shenzhen and Changsha as well as resume services to Fukuoka, Japan from Kuala Lumpur between July and September this year.
Parent company Malaysia Aviation Group (MAG) president and chief executive officer (CEO) Captain Nasaruddin A. Bakar said the new routes will be operated using its Boeing B737-8 aircraft.
He added that the Fukuoka route will initially be operated using the B737-8 aircraft but the airline may deploy a larger aircraft such as the Airbus A330neo (new engine option) depending on demand and new aircraft delivery schedules.
"We have ordered, in total, 95 aircraft – 40 A330neo and 55 737-8 and 737-10 and we expect deliveries in the next six to seven years up to 2032-2033 and because of the issues that the global industry is facing, in particular with supply chains, we are seeing a little bit of delay in our delivery."
"Hence, today, we are planning for Fukuoka using the B737-8. However, if we have the capacity and once we have the aircraft on time, we should be able to swap accordingly.
"If there is demand, we will change the aircraft," he said at a press conference after announcing the destinations at MATTA Fair 2026.
The inaugural flights to Shenzhen and Changsha will be on July 1 and July 8, respectively while Fukuoka will be on Sept 2.
The launch of Changsha and Shenzhen brings Malaysia Airlines' network in China to nine destinations.
The national carrier also flies to Beijing, Shanghai, Guangzhou, Xiamen, Hong Kong, Taipei and Chengdu Tianfu in China.
MAG chief commercial officer for airline business Dersenish Aresandiran said ticket sales for the new services start today with Changha and Shenzhen offered at promotional fares during the MATTA Fair 2026 starting from RM1,088, while Fukuoka fares start from RM1,788.
"This expansion is an important strategic move that strengthen our network and position Malaysia as a key gateway into Asia.
"It reflects our broader strategy to strengthen Kuala Lumpur's position as a key gateway to Asia and beyond while supporting Malaysia's vision for tourism aspiration under Visit Malaysia Year 2026," he said.
Malaysia Airlines will also increase flight frequencies from Kuala Lumpur to Brisbane, Manila and Colombo to meet rising demand and support growing tourism and trade links.
The airline will also operate ad-hoc Kuala Lumpur-London flights on April 18 and 22 to accommodate passengers affected by disruptions involving Middle Eastern carriers.
Meanwhile, MAG is returning as official airline partner and premier sponsor of MATTA Fair 2026 where the group will showcase its next-generation travel technology at its 46,000 sq ft pavilion.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 17 under MCMC probe for spreading false information on fuel, electricity tariffs
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) is investigating 17 people suspected of spreading false information about fuel price hikes and new electricity tariffs on social media.
In a statement, MCMC said as of today, statements have been recorded from eight suspects.
This includes five cases related to false information about fuel price hikes and three cases concerning the new electricity tariffs.
"The remaining nine are under further monitoring and will be called in to have their statements recorded in the near future," the commission said.
MCMC said the investigation is being conducted under Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998, which carries a maximum penalty of RM500,000, imprisonment of up to two years, or both upon conviction.
[[nid:1409443]]
The commission said it views seriously any misuse of digital platforms to mislead the public and warned that firm action will be taken against those found to have violated the law.
"Such dissemination can create panic and anxiety among users. Strict action will be taken against anyone found violating existing laws," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ramanan urges MTUC to resolve internal rifts over Gig Workers Act
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Human Resources Minister Datuk Seri Ramanan Ramakrishnan has urged the Malaysian Trades Union Congress (MTUC) to settle its internal leadership disputes before accusing the government of failing to engage stakeholders over the Gig Workers Act.
This follows a call by MTUC and several NGOs to postpone the enforcement of the Act (Act 872), citing a purported lack of meaningful consultation with workers' representatives.
MTUC secretary-general Kamarul Baharin Mansor had earlier claimed that the union had sent a formal request for a delay to the Human Resources Ministry but had yet to receive a response.
"Despite our repeated efforts since last year, there has been no engagement from the ministry," Kamarul said during a joint press conference with gig worker NGOs.
However, Ramanan said the government had conducted nearly 400 engagement sessions with various stakeholders over the past year to ensure the legislation was comprehensive.
He attributed the union's conflicting narratives to internal friction, which he said had left the organisation divided.
"MTUC has historically dealt with 'Team A' and 'Team B' dynamics. It is challenging to manage the process when they cannot reach an internal consensus; one side supports a move while the other opposes it," Ramanan said today.
"These groups did not just magically appear. It is ironic that a small faction within MTUC claims there was no engagement, whereas the MTUC president himself stood in support of the bill during parliamentary debates," he said.
Ramanan said that the government remains undeterred by the union's internal politics as it advances the historic implementation of the Gig Workers Act 2025 (Act 872).
The act is designed to provide a legal safety net for 1.64 million gig workers, transitioning them from the unprotected informal sector to a regulated environment with mandatory social security and health benefits.
"Today is a historic day for the digital workforce. We are focused on what has been achieved, a massive step forward that provides security and peace of mind for millions of Malaysians," Ramanan said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt plans mediation centre to help B40, M40 resolve disputes
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Putrajaya is considering establishing a Malaysia Mediation Centre (MMC) to expand access to justice for the B40 and M40 income groups.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said said the cross-ministry initiative aims to support small contractors, freelancers, and service providers such as videographers and event planners who face payment disputes.
"Many people cannot afford legal services, such as lawyers or letters of demand, which are costly. A letter of demand typically costs around RM300 to RM500 from a law firm. It is expensive, and when it comes to legal aid, not everyone qualifies, as it is generally limited to those in the B40 category," she said.
Azalina added that the initiative will also assist M40 individuals — for example, young entrepreneurs who are degree holders but are not financially well-off. "Given the current economic challenges, we hope to establish the Malaysia Mediation Centre so that more of these cases can be addressed," she said at the Lawyering 2030 conference today.
The Malaysia Mediation Centre is intended to provide an accessible and affordable solution to resolve disputes amid current economic challenges.
"The government is considering the establishment of the Malaysia Mediation Centre under the Legal Aid Department, supported by a legal framework provided under the Legal Aid and Public Defence Act 2025. Three key priorities have been identified. First, to expand access to mediation services, particularly for the B40 and M40 groups, enabling everyday disputes affecting daily life to be resolved faster, cost-effectively, and in a less adversarial manner," Azalina said.
She added that the initiative will also expand the pool of mediators from the legal profession, including private sector lawyers, retired judges, former legal officers, and other qualified professionals.
The programme will include structured training and capacity building for mediators under the MMC register, as well as across ministries and agencies. It will involve courts, financial and banking institutions, insolvency and credit management agencies, sector-specific tribunals, and regulatory bodies, with support from universities and subject matter experts.
Azalina also said that the Asian International Arbitration Centre (AIAC) will launch a commercial mediation initiative, offering dispute resolution for commercial issues valued at RM250,000 and below. However, she noted that the initiative is subject to cabinet approval.
"The proposed centre would coordinate mediation efforts across ministries, including those handling unity, housing, and consumer affairs, by standardising training and registration and leveraging existing legal provisions. It would also tap into the expertise of retired judges and former civil servants, who would be trained as mediators given their experience," she added.
She highlighted that mediation can be conducted online to save costs. "Proceedings should be conducted online, as costs are high. Travel, parking, waiting, and even utilities such as air-conditioning and lighting all add to the expense," she said.
Azalina added that the centres would initially be located in Kuala Lumpur and Putrajaya.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Survival mode: Kelantan villagers forced to dig wells amid frequent water disruptions
Article:
RANTAU PANJANG: Since January, persistent water supply disruptions have forced villagers in Kampung Banggol Tok Mokyong, a community of about 2,000 people, to dig tube wells manually to access underground water.
For 64-year-old Ariffin Ismail, the decision was born out of necessity after repeated interruptions left his household struggling to meet basic needs.
"We have been facing water problems for months. Sometimes there is no supply for two to three days straight. For bathing, we have to go to the Golok River, while drinking water has to be bought from shops," the odd-job worker said.
The high cost of installing a proper tube well system — between RM5,000 and RM10,000 — made it impossible for low-income villagers to hire contractors.
Ariffin spent about RM1,000 on pipes and basic equipment to build a simple well himself.
"Some villagers in neighbouring villages, including Kampung Tok Deh, spent up to RM11,000 to build a proper tube well. I simply cannot afford that, so I had to rely on my own effort to get it done," he said.
Photos show two men manually driving a pipe deep into the ground with a heavy tool.
It is a tough job that requires strength and endurance, especially in hot, dry conditions.
Another photo shows Ariffin, in a red shirt, holding and steadying a pipe during the work.
He appears to be carefully guiding it to keep it straight while it is driven into the ground.
Using a manual method, Ariffin hammered pipes up to 9m into the ground to reach water.
"If the soil is hard, it is difficult, but if it is clay, the work becomes easier," he said.
He admitted the water quality could not be guaranteed.
"We are doing this just to get water first. Whether it is clean or not comes second because survival is the priority now," he said.
Housewife Surani Daud, 48, a mother of six, said her family had little choice but to adapt.
"The supply is often cut off. We cannot depend on it fully, especially with a big family like mine. I have to find alternatives, so I decided to install pipes into the ground to get water," she said.
Surani hopes authorities will upgrade the water system to provide reliable access.
"We just want a reliable supply. Water is a basic necessity, and it should not be this difficult," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: A-G explains murder charge for fatal drink-driving crash
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Attorney-General (A-G) Tan Sri Mohd Dusuki Mokhtar has defended the decision to charge a driver with murder in the fatal drink-driving crash in Klang, citing evidence the act was "so imminently dangerous" that it would, in all probability, cause death.
Dusuki said there were sufficient elements to support a charge under Section 300(d) of the Penal Code against R. Saktygaanapathy, based on the circumstances surrounding the incident.
He highlighted the accused's deliberate act of driving into the opposite lane at high speed, which created a highly perilous situation for the victim.
"The act of the accused entering the opposing lane intentionally and at high speed resulted in a situation that was 'so imminently dangerous' to the deceased, as provided under Section 300(d) of the Penal Code," he said.
Section 300(d) defines murder as an act committed with knowledge that it is so imminently dangerous it will, in all probability, cause death or bodily injury likely to cause death, and is carried out without justification for taking such a risk.
The A-G said the accused's actions leading up to the crash were corroborated by witness testimonies and dashcam footage from a vehicle at the scene.
"The evidence from witnesses and dashcam recordings confirms the conduct of the accused at the time of the incident," he added.
On March 29, Saktygaanapathy was charged at the Klang magistrate's court with murder over the death of delivery rider Amirul Hafiz Omar, who died in a head-on collision along Jalan Raya Barat on the same day.
According to court documents, Saktygaanapathy allegedly drove against traffic at high speed before colliding head-on with Amirul, who was riding a motorcycle. The impact flung the victim onto another vehicle, and he was pronounced dead at the scene.
In a separate case, Saktygaanapathy pleaded guilty to self-administering drugs, namely benzodiazepine and tetrahydrocannabinol (THC).
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Eight arrested for using fake bank slips to purchase goods via WhatsApp
Article:
TEMERLOH: Police have arrested eight men suspected of being part of a syndicate that used fake bank transfer slips to purchase goods via WhatsApp.
The suspects were detained in a raid in Cheras, Selangor, following intelligence gathered by the Pahang Commercial Crime Investigation Department and the Temerloh police headquarters.
Temerloh police chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Nasyim Bahron, said the arrests followed a report by a 33-year-old saleswoman who claimed losses of RM7,575 after being deceived by the syndicate.
"In the March 25 incident, the victim received an order for engine oil from the suspects and delivered the goods, unaware that payment had not been made. Investigations found that the suspects posed as customers and sent fake bank slips to convince the victim that the transaction had been completed," he said.
He added that police seized 11 mobile phones, a lorry, and a set of vehicle keys believed to have been used in the crime during the raid.
All suspects are being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code, which carries a jail term of up to 10 years, whipping, and a fine upon conviction.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Refiners expected to cut runs as hedgable economics drive decisions
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Refiners are expected to reduce operating runs as decisions are driven by hedgable economics rather than directional market views, said independent research and energy intelligence company Rystad Energy.
Its chief oil analyst, Paola Rodriguez-Masiu, said while nothing appears structurally broken for refiners on paper, conditions on the ground point to a strained Asian refining market scrambling for additional barrels.
"The forward curve is telling them to 'run', but the arithmetic of crude calculated on a landed basis at the refinery wipes out the screen margin completely.
"As refineries do not operate on directional views but on hedgable economics, the rational response, the only financially defensible response, is to cut runs," she said.
She noted that European second-quarter 2026 refinery margins are holding at around US$30 per barrel for hydrocracking and coking configurations, and about US$20 per barrel for fluid catalytic cracking, with a relatively flat forward structure.
However, in the physical market, West African grades are being offered at Brent plus US$20 to US$30 per barrel on a free-on-board basis.
"From the Niger Delta to Brazil, every available replacement barrel is being offered at levels that effectively consume the entire forward refining margin before the crude even arrives.
"Asian refiners, shut out of Middle Eastern supply, are bidding aggressively for every available Atlantic Basin barrel," she said.
Rodriguez-Masiu added that European and South Korean refiners are now competing for the same Egina cargo, Forcados lifting, and Brazilian Tupi crude.
At current physical differentials and freight rates, European refiners buying spot crude cannot make money running those barrels.
"The core issue is simple: the financial market is not just lagging the physical market; it is making the supply crisis structurally worse, and the refiners that are being forced to cut runs today will not be able to restart overnight when the signal finally arrives.
"Restarting capacity is neither immediate nor frictionless," she added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump announces destruction of Iran's tallest bridge
Article:
WASHINGTON: US President Donald Trump said Thursday the tallest bridge in Iran had been destroyed, hours after threatening to bomb the country "back to the Stone Ages."
Ghodratollah Seif, the deputy governor of Alborz province, where the B1 bridge is located, said the strike had killed eight people and wounded 95 others,according to state TV and Fars news agency.
Due to reporting restrictions, AFP is not able to access the sites of strikes nor to independently verify tolls in Iran.
Trump posted footage on social media of smoke rising from the B1 bridge in Karaj, around 20 miles (35 kilometers) southwest of Tehran -- and warned that there would be further destruction unless Iran comes to the table to end the five-week war.
"The biggest bridge in Iran comes tumbling down, never to be used again - Much more to follow! IT IS TIME FOR IRAN TO MAKE A DEAL BEFORE IT IS TOO LATE, AND THERE IS NOTHING LEFT OF WHAT STILL COULD BECOME A GREAT COUNTRY!" Trump said.
Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said in a post on X: "Striking civilian structures, including unfinished bridges, will not compel Iranians to surrender.
"It only conveys the defeat and moral collapse of an enemy in disarray."
Iranian state television had earlier reported two US-Israeli strikes on the bridge.
"A few minutes ago, the American-Zionist enemy once again targeted the B1 bridge in Karaj," state TV said, adding that an initial strike had caused two civilian casualties.
It said the later attack took place as emergency teams were deployed to the site to help victims of the first strike.
The B1 bridge, which was still under construction, is the Middle East's tallest according to Fars/Iranian media, with a 447-foot (136-meter) column.
Trump delivered a speech Wednesday in which he argued that the war launched by the United States and Israel on February 28 was almost over -- even as he threatened to bomb Iran "extremely hard" if it didn't bow to his demands.
"Over the next two to three weeks, we are going to bring them back to the Stone Ages, where they belong," he said in his first prime-time address to the nation on the war.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Will the Mideast conflict lead to a new international order?
Article:
THE Iran war, with unclear and even changing objectives, takes the world into uncharted territory.
It used to be that when the United States waged war, it was couched in high principles such as advancing democracy, never mind if this was actually achieved or not. With this latest war, the US has even dispensed with the pretence of obtaining the sanction of the United Nations.
What President Donald Trump has unapologetically done in Iran and earlier in Venezuela is to assert that might is right and so-called international rules be damned.
"The violations that are being committed today by all sides in the Middle East go beyond violation of any particular agreement on international humanitarian law or any provision in the United Nations Charter.
"What we are seeing rather is the collapse of an entire structure of expectations that, since 1945, has kept modern conflicts within limits."
So asserts Professor Randy David in the Philippine Daily Inquirer.
This is most regrettable since modern-day international rules have been largely the legacy of US leadership in the post-World War 2 world.
Even as the US saw it fit to have rules for how other nations conduct themselves, while it sometimes placed itself above and outside these rules, its leaders mostly understood that it is in its own enlightened self-interest for all to submit to the same rules.
A world bereft of rules by which all nations abide is a rather slippery slope towards anarchy in which malign forces — be they state or non-state actors — will thrive.
When free and safe passage through international waterways is threatened, international shipping and commerce may eventually slow or grind to a halt.
The world has undergone remarkable economic growth and prosperity in the decades since 1945 precisely because there are rules underwritten by global bodies, with the active or passive support of the major powers, principally the US.
If the US believes it can now act as it pleases with complete impunity, the rest of the world may increasingly look towards others to step forward to safeguard a rules-based international order, ironically a turn of phrase that, until now, was strongly identified with the US-led West.
Before the current global order is further and irreparably eroded, it is incumbent on other major countries to step up to defend it since it is in their and every other country's interests to do so.
Many eyes will be looking towards China, a major beneficiary of the existing order and presumably one with an abiding interest in preserving it.
It will be interesting to watch the interaction between Chinese President Xi Jinping and Trump when the latter visits China in the coming month.
China has so far shown no willingness to take up the heavy burdens of global leadership that Trump seems to so cavalierly want to give up.
Will there instead be a new modus vivendi arrived at by Xi and Trump as the two countries — often now described as peer rivals — continue to size each other up and postpone hard questions about any new global order to another time?
These are indeed interesting times, but we must hope there is no sting in the tail that usually comes with such a phrase.
* The writer views developments in the nation, region and the wider world from his vantage point in Kuching
*The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: HLIB trims Malaysia's 2026 GDP to 4.5pct on global energy crisis
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Hong Leong Investment Bank Bhd (HLIB) trimmed its 2026 gross domestic product (GDP) to 4.5 per cent from 4.7 per cent following the global energy crisis resulting from the conflict in the Middle East.
It said growth is expected to be supported by strong electric and electronic exports and continued consumption activity.
In a research note today, HLIB said that although Malaysian vessels were recently granted free passage through the Strait of Hormuz, crude oil supply risks cannot be fully discounted.
It noted that Malaysia's position as a net oil and gas exporter (2025: RM18 billion) is primarily driven by LNG (RM45 billion) and, to a smaller extent, refined petroleum products (RM3 billion).
"Importantly, its refined petroleum surplus is dependent on crude oil as a feedstock, for which Malaysia is a net importer, amounting to RM30 billion, with an estimated 50-60 per cent of this input sourced externally, 70 per cent of which originates from the Middle East.
"Consequently, after factoring in the temporary energy supply disruption, we have trimmed our 2026 GDP growth forecast from 4.7 per cent year-on-year to 4.5 per cent," it said.
Meanwhile, HLIB anticipates some upward price pressures on RON97, unsubsidised diesel, electricity, as well as food from higher commodity costs amid unfavourable weather conditions and raised the 2026 Consumer Price Index (CPI) forecast to 2.0 per cent year-on-year from 1.7 per cent.
"With inflation risks tilted to the upside and growth prospects moderating, we expect Bank Negara Malaysia to adopt a cautious, wait-and-see approach, keeping pat on the overnight policy rate at 2.75 per cent as it assesses the evolving impact of the Iran war," it added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Fiscal support, stronger ringgit to cushion Middle East headwinds: Kenanga IB
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Headwinds from the Middle East conflict are expected to be eased by fiscal support, including Sumbangan Tunai Rahmah (STR) and Sumbangan Asas Rahmah (SARA), a stronger ringgit, and resilience in food and beverage spending, Kenanga Investment Bank Bhd (Kenanga IB) said.
In a research note, the investment bank said essential spending is taking priority and believes consumption is likely to remain skewed towards necessities in the near term, resulting in softer discretionary spending should cost pressures intensify.
"This aligns with our in-house view that inflation could trend higher amid the Middle East Asia conflict, with our calendar year 2026 inflation forecast revised to 2.1 per cent from 1.9 per cent.
"While energy subsidies and a stronger ringgit should continue to anchor prices, potential disruptions to oil and fertiliser supply chains may lift food and transport costs, thereby eroding household purchasing power," Kenanga IB said.
From a portfolio perspective, the investment bank said heavyweight names remain broadly neutral.
However, it is selectively positive on specific counters with better earnings resilience, defensive, essentials-led exposure, strong fundamentals, and value-oriented positioning.
Hence, it said consumption remains resilient, but spending is still cautious and selective, with consumers prioritising essential and value-for-money purchases.
This is reflected in full-year sub-sector trends, where mini-markets and convenience stores outperformed (+13 per cent), while more discretionary segments such as furniture, home improvement, and electrical goods contracted (-10 per cent), indicating continued deferment of non-essential spending.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 5E Resources set for earnings growth on high barriers, ESG strength, says RHB Research
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Waste management company 5E Resources Holdings Bhd is expected to see strong earnings growth, according to RHB Research.
The firm said this is supported by 5E's high entry barriers, robust profit margins, and solid environmental, social, and governance (ESG) credentials.
The company plans to raise RM79.2 million from its initial public offering (IPO), primarily to fund an expansion in Perak aimed at tapping the under-served northern West Malaysia market.
More than 70 per cent of the IPO proceeds will go to the Perak facility, which is expected to more than double its waste-handling capacity by 2030 while reaching new industrial customers in the northern region.
Its Johor facility, scheduled to start operations in the second half of 2026, will increase the company's annual waste-handling capacity by about 80 per cent.
RHB Research noted that this new facility will introduce advanced recovery technologies and expand 5E's licensed waste categories.
5E provides end-to-end services for industrial hazardous waste, including collection, recovery, and recycling.
With 18 years of experience, it serves over 500 customers and is licensed to manage nearly half of Malaysia's scheduled waste categories.
Its participation in high-profile projects such as the Kim Kim River clean-up demonstrates both technical expertise and regulatory trust.
The company's business model allows it to pass fuel and electricity costs to clients, while also generating additional value by recovering materials such as oils, solvents, and chemicals for reuse or sale.
RHB Research said 5E is well-positioned under the 13th Malaysia Plan, with steady demand from electronics, chemical, and metal industries.
The group maintains a healthy balance sheet, with net cash of RM49.3 million and minimal borrowings of RM1.8 million as of October 2025.
While 5E does not have a formal dividend policy, it has consistently paid dividends over the past three years.
RHB Research forecasts a 30 per cent payout ratio, translating into dividend yields of 1.7 per cent, 2.3 per cent, and 2.8 per cent for financial years 2025 to 2027 (FY25-FY27).
The firm also projects 5E to achieve a three-year earnings compound annual growth rate of 20.3 per cent between FY24 and FY27.
RHB Research noted that the growth is expected to be driven by the completion of the Johor facility and sustained demand for waste management services.
Margins are projected to remain steady or trend higher, supported by cost savings and scale benefits from improved operating leverage.
While there are no direct listed peers in Malaysia, the firm cited Tex Cycle Technology (M) Bhd and Hiap Huat Holdings Bhd as partially exposed to waste recovery, though these segments represent only a small portion of their earnings.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Eskayvie targets regional growth as global wellness market expands
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The global wellness industry, including Malaysia, continues to experience strong post-pandemic expansion as consumers increasingly prioritise preventive healthcare, immunity support, and mental wellbeing.
Driven by this structural shift in consumer behaviour, the health and wellness sector has evolved into a multi-trillion-ringgit global economy, with demand accelerating across supplements, functional foods, skincare, and lifestyle-based preventive care solutions.
Riding this wave is Eskayvie, a homegrown halal-certified health and wellness brand, which is positioning itself for aggressive regional expansion to capture emerging opportunities across Southeast Asia.
Founder Dr Kartika Wati Mohamed said the company is strengthening its regional footprint to enhance competitiveness in an increasingly crowded and innovation-driven wellness landscape.
Eskayvie is targeting key Southeast Asian markets, including Indonesia, Thailand, the Philippines, Vietnam, Cambodia, Singapore, and Brunei, all identified for their rising health awareness, expanding middle-class populations, and increasing demand for preventive wellness solutions.
"These markets have been strategically selected based on their strong growth potential in the health and wellness sector, increasing consumer awareness on preventive healthcare and the opportunity to build sustainable entrepreneurial ecosystems through Eskayvie Nexus," she said in a statement.
Dr Kartika noted that Indonesia remains a priority market, given its large population base and strong alignment with Eskayvie's product portfolio and business model.
In the short term, the company is targeting sustained double-digit annual growth while reinforcing its leadership in Malaysia and accelerating expansion in selected regional markets.
Over the medium term, Eskayvie aims to scale towards a multi-hundred-million-ringgit revenue milestone, supported by a more diversified regional presence and deeper market penetration.
"Ultimately our goal is not just rapid expansion, but sustainable and profitable growth by balancing revenue acceleration with strong cost discipline, ecosystem strength and long-term brand value," she said.
Eskayvie's growth strategy is anchored on product innovation, distribution expansion, and the integration of data, technology, and operational excellence to enhance efficiency and consumer engagement.
Originally focused on supplements, Eskayvie has broadened its portfolio to include skincare and functional health products, targeting both beauty-conscious and health-focused consumer segments.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Alcohol, drugs push road deaths to 31 last year
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Fatal road accidents involving drivers under the influence of alcohol and drugs have risen over the past two years.
Traffic Enforcement and Investigation Department statistics show 27 fatal cases in 2024, increasing to 31 last year.
Its director, Datuk Seri Mohammed Hasbullah Ali, said the rise is small, but each case involves serious loss of life and should not be taken lightly.
"The main challenge is not only enforcement on the ground, but also raising awareness, responsibility and moral values among road users," he told Berita Harian yesterday.
At the same time, the police will increase roadblocks and step up integrated operations with other agencies to detect and act against drivers under the influence of alcohol.
He said roadblocks and random screening tests are among the most effective ways to curb drunk driving.
"To strengthen enforcement, the traffic enforcement unit will intensify roadblocks, especially during high-risk periods such as at night and on weekends.
"Integrated operations with other agencies will also be enhanced to ensure more comprehensive and effective action.
"Roadblocks and random screening tests have proven effective in identifying offenders and creating a psychological deterrent, while the consistent presence of authorities at high-risk locations and times improves compliance with the law," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Govt vows probe into alleged abuse at unregistered children's home
Article:
BATU CAVES: The government has pledged a thorough investigation into alleged abuse at an unregistered children's home in Taman Selayang Permata, as more victims continue to come forward.
Women, Family and Community Development Minister Datuk Seri Nancy Shukri said enforcement action had been taken after authorities discovered the facility was operating illegally.
She said the Gombak district social welfare office acted promptly following a report lodged on March 30.
"An investigation found the facility was unregistered and operating outside the provisions of the Care Centres Act 1993. Enforcement action was taken immediately upon this discovery," she said in a statement today.
Nancy added that all children from the home had since been placed in safe care, either returned to their families or placed under appropriate guardianship.
"Those requiring medical attention have received and continue to receive treatment. The ministry is actively cooperating with the Malaysian Police Force's D11 division to ensure this case is pursued to its full extent under the law," she said.
She said that further details would not be disclosed at this stage to avoid compromising investigations and to protect the welfare and privacy of the children.
"The ministry's priority is the safety and wellbeing of every child. We will continue to monitor this case closely," she said, urging the public to report any unregistered childcare centres to the nearest social welfare department office.
The case has widened, with three teenage girls aged 16 to 17 alleging they were assaulted in separate incidents linked to individuals associated with the home's operator.
A source said police received three additional reports from the teenagers, including two alleging molestation and one claiming rape.
The suspects, believed to have posed as volunteers and stayed at the home, are now at large.
Investigators are also examining potential links between the home and another facility, an old folks' home located about 7km away, allegedly operated by the same individual, who is now the primary suspect.
Pertubuhan Kebangkitan Tiga Tangan Selangor (PKTTS) president S. Maran said the latest disclosures suggested a wider network of individuals may have had access to the children.
"This is not just a failure; this is a breakdown of basic safeguards," he said, calling for a comprehensive probe into all who had dealings with the home.
He also urged authorities to conduct medical and psychological assessments on all current and former residents, warning that more victims could still be suffering in silence.
The home, which housed 36 children, came under scrutiny after multiple minors aged nine to 17 were found to have been abused over a prolonged period. At least nine children are believed to have been assaulted, with five admitted to hospital for further examination.
Police have arrested four individuals, including the 31-year-old operator, who had allegedly been running the home for nearly a decade without registration.
The suspects have been remanded until Friday as investigations continue, while authorities have ordered the immediate closure of the facility and secured the premises.
Efforts are ongoing to relocate affected children to safer environments or reunite them with their families.
On Thursday, the New Straits Times
reported
that Bukit Aman's Sexual, Women and Child Investigation Division D11 principal assistant director Senior Assistant Commissioner Siti Kamsiah Hassan confirmed an investigation is ongoing into the allegations involving the children's home.
Police have yet to confirm if additional arrests will be made following the latest revelations. The four suspects' remand, which ends today, is also expected to be extended following new allegations made by the home's residents.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Carrying humanity's stories into the cosmos
Article:
LETTER:
When NASA launches a mission to the Moon, it is often framed as a triumph of science, engineering, and national ambition. Rockets rise, trajectories are calculated, and humanity once again reaches outward.
Yet, beneath the spectacle lies a quieter, more enduring question: What does it mean for humanity, to return to the Moon?
Beyond the space community and global media, there is a curious indifference to these lunar missions. The Moon, once the ultimate symbol of wonder, throughout the world from East to West, now competes with the immediacy of life on Earth.
But history reminds us that every great leap in science is also a leap in imagination. Long before spacecraft, the Moon lived in our stories.
'Jikalau tidak karna bintang, Masakan bulan terbit tinggi, Jikalau tidak karna abang, Masakan datang adek ke-mari'
(If not for the stars above, Why would the moon venture high, If not for you, my only love, Why would I venture nigh…)
In the Malay world, it glows through pantun and hikayat - guiding lovers, marking time, bearing witness to longing and destiny. Across civilisations, it has symbolised distance and desire, rhythm and eternity.
The Moon is not new to us. What is new is how we return to it.
The Artemis II mission — a 10-day lunar flyby using the Space Launch System (SLS) rocket and Orion spacecraft — marks humanity's first crewed journey around the Moon since 1972.
It is, undeniably, a technological milestone. But it is also something more. Because this time, we do not go alone.
Onboard Artemis II is a remarkable inclusion: a poetry archive. Through the #LunarCodex, conceived by Samuel Peralta, human creativity travels alongside astronauts.
CODEX RIGEL, part of this mission, follows earlier archives such as CODEX ORION, launched with Artemis I.
Within these archives are voices from across the world - including Malaysians. This is not incidental. It is intentional.
It reflects a quiet but powerful shift in how we define exploration. Space is no longer only a domain of science. It is becoming a space for culture, memory, and identity.
For those of us rooted in storytelling traditions, this moment carries particular resonance.
At Hikayat Fandom, we see in this not just a scientific mission, but a continuation of something far older. For centuries, hikayat has served as a vessel of civilisation - preserving knowledge, encoding philosophy, and shaping identity through narrative.
Now, in a way both poetic and unprecedented, that tradition finds itself aligned with humanity's most advanced endeavour.
We are witnessing the expansion of storytelling - not across kingdoms or seas, but into the cosmos. And this matters.
We live in a time defined by urgency: climate change, cultural erosion, rapid technological disruption. In such a moment, it is easy to question the relevance of space exploration.
But missions like Artemis do more than extend our physical reach. They expand our imaginative one.
They force us to ask: What stories are worth carrying forward? Whose voices will represent humanity beyond Earth? And what does it mean to be human, when we are no longer bound to a single world?
The inclusion of art and literature aboard Artemis II offers a compelling answer.
It tells us that progress is not only measured in distance travelled, but in meaning preserved. That even as we move outward, we remain anchored to the stories that define us.
In this sense, the Moon is no longer just a scientific frontier. It is a cultural one.
The presence of poetry aboard a spacecraft is not symbolic alone - it is declarative. It asserts that humanity does not advance by leaving its humanity behind, but by carrying it forward.
From the hikayat of the Malay Archipelago to the digital archives of the Lunar Codex, we see a shared impulse: to remember, to imagine, and to endure.
Perhaps the world may seem indifferent. But some of us are watching, quietly, with intention. Because we recognise that this is more than a mission.
It is a moment where science and story meet. Where rockets carry not just bodies, but voices. Where the ancient human need to tell stories finds its place among the stars.
From Artemis to Orion, and beyond, humanity is not merely exploring space. We are extending our narrative.
NINOT AZIZ
Kuala Lumpur
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Swiss man arrested at KLIA for smuggling 3.32kg of heroin
Article:
fuadnizam@nst.com.my
KUALA LUMPUR: A Swiss national's attempt to smuggle 3.32kg of heroin concealed inside a water hose reel was foiled after he was detained upon arrival at Kuala Lumpur International Airport (KLIA).
KLIA Customs Department director Zulkifli Muhammad said the drugs were detected with the assistance of personnel from the Malaysian Border Control and Protection Agency (MCBA).
"KLIA MCBA personnel detained a Swiss national at the arrival hall of Terminal 2. The suspect was attempting to enter Malaysia from a Southeast Asian country.
"He was stopped after a scan of the water hose reel box he was carrying revealed suspicious and questionable images inside," he said in a statement.
Zulkifli said the drugs were subsequently handed over to the KLIA Customs Department Enforcement Division for further action.
The heroin, estimated to be worth RM265,920, was seized, and the suspect was detained to assist investigations under Section 39B(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act for drug trafficking.
If convicted, the offence carries the death penalty or life imprisonment, along with not fewer than 12 strokes of the rotan if the death penalty is not imposed.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Boh joins Warisan Kuala Lumpur Initiative at Sultan Abdul Samad Building
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Boh Plantations has been invited to participate in Khazanah Nasional Bhd's Dana Warisan Kuala Lumpur (Warisan KL) restoration initiative at Bangunan Sultan Abdul Samad, one of Malaysia's most significant, historic and architecturally rich landmarks in the city centre.
Block One of the building complex was officially opened on Feb 2, marking the completion of the initiative's first phase.
The restoration works aim to preserve the building's national heritage character while improving safety standards, accessibility, and the overall visitor experience.
Boh chairman Caroline Russell said the company's participation in Warisan KL at Bangunan Sultan Abdul Samad is especially meaningful, as it allows Boh to celebrate both its own legacy and Malaysia's broader national story, shaped by its people, places, and shared history.
"Our journey in Malaya began in 1890, at a time when this very building symbolised administration, progress and the shaping of a nation," she said in a statement.
"To share Boh's story within a landmark that has witnessed Malaysia's transformation is a powerful reminder that heritage is not static. It is an invaluable treasure we carry forward, nurture and pass on with purpose. We hope visitors will see how heritage lives on, not only in historic walls, but in ideas, values and everyday rituals, such as sharing a cup of tea," she said.
Boh's presence at Bangunan Sultan Abdul Samad reflects a shared history dating back to the formative years of Kuala Lumpur.
The Russell family, founders of Boh, have been part of Malaya's development since 1890, when John Russell, fondly known as "Jack", arrived in the Straits Settlements as Selangor government printer.
Serving under the British Resident in Selangor, he played a key role in documenting early governance, infrastructure and social life in Malaya, when government offices (today known as Bangunan Sultan Abdul Samad) stood at the centre of administrative activity.
From those early beginnings, the family was involved in Malaya's growth across printing, engineering, construction, mining, agriculture and commerce. This legacy continued when J.A. Russell, one of Jack's sons, founded Boh Plantations in 1929, establishing Malaysia's first highland tea garden in the Cameron Highlands and laying the foundation for what would become the country's best-known tea brand.
As part of the Warisan KL initiative, Boh is showcasing curated exhibits tracing its journey from 1890 to the present, offering visitors an immersive look at its heritage and role in Malaysia's development.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Pro-Palestinian flotilla's new Gaza mission to start in Spain on April 12
Article:
BARCELONA: A flotilla of pro-Palestinian activists who attempted to reach Gaza last year said on Thursday they would launch a new mission to the devastated territory from Barcelona on April 12.
The Global Sumud Flotilla's first weeks-long journey across the Mediterranean Sea to Gaza, blockaded by Israel during the war against Palestinian group Hamas, drew worldwide attention.
Israel's interception of their boats and arrests of the activists as they approached Gaza, which suffered severe shortages of food, water, medicine and fuel, sparked international condemnation.
The group, which described its first attempt as a humanitarian mission, said the latest trip starting in Spain's second city would gather more than 80 boats and 1,000 international participants.
"The cost of inaction is too high to bear," it said in a statement, adding that a land-based movement would join the maritime action to create pressure in multiple countries.
"As Gaza endures intensifying blockade, violence, and deprivation, the mission is a principled, nonviolent intervention: a defence of human dignity, a call for humanitarian access, and a demand for international accountability," the group said.
Gaza is under a fragile ceasefire agreed last October, which followed two years of devastating conflict sparked by the October 7, 2023 Hamas attack on Israel.
The attack resulted in the deaths of 1,221 people Israel, mostly civilians, according to official Israeli figures tallied by AFP. Palestinian groups also abducted 251 hostages.
The retaliatory Israeli military campaign killed more than 70,000 people in Gaza, also mostly civilians, according to the Hamas-run territory's health ministry whose figures the United Nations considers reliable.
Both sides have repeatedly accused each other of violating the ceasefire.
Gaza's health ministry says Israeli strikes have killed more than 700 Palestinians since the truce. Israel says five of its soldiers have been killed in the same period. - AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: NGOs lack confidence in AG-PP appointment process, says Azalina
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Non-governmental organisations have expressed a lack of confidence in the proposed appointment process under Putrajaya's bill to separate the roles of the Attorney-General and Public Prosecutor.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said said the government will review the feedback and refine the bill to ensure the proposed constitutional amendment secures a two-thirds majority in Parliament.
"The issue raised is a lack of confidence in the existing appointment process, and possibly in the constitutional framework itself.
"So, we need to study the matter.
"As the minister responsible for legal affairs and institutional reform, I am very open to these views," she said after speaking at the Lawyering 2030 conference here today.
Azalina said cessation of employment was also among the issues raised.
"I can sense a great deal of suspicion – a sense of doubt in many forms, including doubts about this approach and that approach.
"However, we must not forget that our country is bound by the constitution," she added.
She said the feedback would be considered by the parliamentary special select committee.
The government must secure a two-thirds majority for the historic change, making compromise with other politicians and parties essential, she said.
"Every political party, particularly the different political blocs, will have their own benchmarks.
"For Umno and Barisan Nasional, we are very clear in our understanding.
"However, other parties may have their own perspectives, representing the voices of their supporters and stakeholders," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Paris police ban French Muslim gathering
Article:
PARIS: The Paris police on Thursday banned an annual gathering of French Muslims north of the capital, citing a "major terrorist risk" that it might be targeted.
The Annual Gathering of the Muslims of France had been due to take place at Bourget, just north of Paris, from Friday until Monday.
The order banning it issued by the Paris police said there was a risk that "small far-right groups" might try to disrupt the event.
But the police order said the event was "taking place in a particularly tense international and national context."
The gathering was "exposed to a significant terrorist risk targeting the Muslim community", it added.
The order cited a foiled bid last weekend to bomb the Bank of America building in Paris – an event, it said, that underlined the seriousness of the threat inside France.
French prosecutors said the attempted attack might have been linked to a pro-Iran group, as security fears flare over the war in the Middle East.
Police also cited a polarised political debate during municipal elections last month.
The decision comes as the French interior ministry prepares a draft law designed to tackle the danger of radical Islamist elements infiltrating Muslim groups.
The bill is due to be presented to President Emmanuel Macron's cabinet at the end of April, the ministry told AFP, confirming a report by Le Parisien newspaper.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kedah siblings forced out of school due to poverty
Article:
KUALA NERANG: Poverty has kept two siblings in Naka out of school, leaving them without formal education.
Muhamad Rizhan Rizqi, 9, and Muhamad Raiqal Rayyan, 8, who should be in Year Two and Three, spend their days at home.
Their mother, Siti Nur Jamilah Jamaludin, 39, said their situation worsened after her mother's death and her husband left several years ago.
"I want them to go to school, but I didn't know how to handle everything. If there is help, I am ready to send them," she said at their home in Taman Naka Jaya.
The single mother said she feels guilty seeing her children miss out on education and often ask when they can attend school like their friends.
Kuala Nerang, a small rural town in Kedah's Padang Terap district, is surrounded by padi farms, vegetable plots, and livestock holdings.
Siti also said she used to run a food business, which stopped during the Covid-19 pandemic.
She now earns about RM30 to RM40 a day helping a friend run a business.
She added their home has no water or electricity, forcing them to rely on candles and torchlights at night.
Her phone must be charged elsewhere using a power bank, and neighbours occasionally provide food.
Meanwhile, Kedah Anak Muda Madani chairman Datuk Zuraidi Rahim said he was shocked by the case and called it unacceptable.
"Upon learning of it, I immediately visited with teachers and contacted the District Education Office and the State Education Department for urgent action," he said.
"We will enrol both children in a daily school to help them adjust gradually, and I will take them in as my foster children."
He added that all schooling needs would be provided and the home's water and electricity issues resolved.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Penang launches solar project to cut carbon footprint, boost employment
Article:
NIBONG TEBAL: Penang today launched the groundbreaking of the Large-Scale Solar 5 (LSS5) project in Byram, a major initiative to reduce the state's carbon footprint while creating skilled employment opportunities.
Set to commence commercial operations in July 2027, LSS5 — with a capacity of 29.99 megawatt-peak (MWp) and an RM103 million investment — will supply clean energy and strengthen the electricity grid in Nibong Tebal and surrounding areas.
Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow said the shift to solar energy was crucial in tackling climate change.
"This project will not only provide sustainable power but also support high-tech jobs and economic growth in Penang," he said in a speech at the project site this morning.
Chow said the ability to deliver a stable and green energy supply would continue to attract high-technology investments into the state.
He added that the Byram project would complement Penang's existing green energy initiatives, including LSS4 in Seberang Prai Tengah, solar developments in Bukit Bendera, as well as planned floating solar projects at Mengkuang Dam and Teluk Bahang.
"The project also aligns with Penang2030's smart urban services integration vision, utilising smart energy systems, real-time monitoring, and data analytics," he added.
Chow noted that the initiative had become increasingly important following the enforcement of the Energy Efficiency and Conservation Act (EECA) on Jan 1, 2025, which requires large industrial and commercial energy users to adopt stricter energy-saving measures.
"LSS5 will help drive these sectors towards achieving national energy-saving targets of up to 22 per cent by 2040," he said.
Chow said that Malaysia had surpassed its 31 per cent renewable energy target ahead of schedule, achieving 32 per cent by the end of 2025, and is on track for 40 per cent by 2035 and net-zero emissions by 2050.
He also praised Penang Development Corporation's subsidiary, Solar Voltech Sdn Bhd, and its strategic partner Solarvest Holdings Berhad for overseeing the engineering, procurement, construction, and commissioning (EPCC) works, noting how public-private partnerships can drive Penang's clean energy transformation.
"This is not just about building a solar farm, but investing in Penang's future as a resilient and modern economy while safeguarding the environment for future generations," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Matrade secures over RM200mil in trade deals at China food and drinks fair
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Malaysia External Trade Development Corporation (Matrade), in collaboration with Messe Worldwide Sdn Bhd, has secured more than RM200 million in trade value at the recently concluded China Food and Drinks Fair (CFDF) in Sichuan, China.
Held from March 26 to 28, the event featured nine Malaysian small and medium enterprises (SMEs) under Majlis Amanah Rakyat's (Mara) Gate to Global Programme.
The Malaysian Pavillion hosted over 60 targeted business meetings, connecting exhibitors with high-profile buyers from across China and generating total sales exceeding RM200 million.
CFDF is considered to be China's longest-running and most influential trade exhibition, serving as a powerful gateway for global food and beverage brands to tap into the world's largest consumer market.
Matrade chief executive officer Abu Bakar Yusof said, "Matrade's participation in CFDF is in line with its export market diversification strategy by exploring the growing Tier 2 and Tier 3 cities in China, such as Chengdu."
"The trade event emerged as a key gateway to the fast-growing Western China market and represents a significant opportunity for Malaysia's premium food and beverage products."
China and Malaysia's bilateral trade in 2025 hit RM541.54 billion, with the Sichuan province emerging as one of the country's top 10 provincial trading partners within China, accounting for RM18.85 billion of the total trade volume.
The strong performance at CFDF underscores the resilience and global appeal of Malaysian food and beverage brands, further setting a promising tone for future bilateral trade.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MARKET PULSE AM APRIL 3, 2026 [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Bursa Malaysia opened higher today, tracking a mixed performance on Wall Street after US President Donald Trump's latest televised address offered no clear resolution to the Middle East conflict.
While valuations appear increasingly attractive at current levels, traders are largely staying on the sidelines as they await greater clarity.
For now, the index is expected to move within the 1,690 to 1,705 range.
In the crypto market, Bitcoin fell to RM269,686 amid ongoing tensions.
Ethereum rose to RM8,314 while Solana was up at RM321.
That's it for Market Pulse.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Geopolitical tensions threaten FMCG packaging supply, says CIMB Securities
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A prolonged disruption stemming from geopolitical tensions could tighten packaging availability across the fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG) sector, beyond just Farm Fresh Bhd, according to CIMB Securities Sdn Bhd.
In a note, CIMB Securities said Farm Fresh has experienced disruptions to its high-density polyethylene (HDPE) resin supply chain over the past 10 to 14 days, following the US-Iran conflict.
This has affected production of its one-litre and two-litre plastic bottles, which account for about 12 to 13 per cent of the group's sales.
To mitigate the impact, the group has shifted production to gable-top paper cartons, which contribute roughly eight to nine per cent of sales and currently have spare capacity to offset potential losses from plastic bottle formats.
Farm Fresh indicated that paper-based packaging supply remains sufficient for now, although it is facing modest cost inflation.
CIMB Securities noted that the direct impact of the conflict is already filtering through in the form of higher packaging costs, particularly HDPE, as well as increased diesel-linked logistics expenses and potentially electricity costs.
These factors could affect about eight to ten per cent of the group's total cost base.
Beyond these immediate pressures, Farm Fresh has flagged potential second-order risks if the conflict persists.
While the group has forward-hedged a significant portion of its key inputs, prolonged disruptions could drive up prices of dairy ingredients, fertiliser and animal feed, while further escalating logistics and packaging costs.
The group expects its core dairy segment to remain relatively resilient, supported by the essential nature of its products, with stable demand anticipated through the fourth quarter of financial year 2026 (FY26).
However, downside risks remain if inflationary pressures intensify and consumers scale back discretionary spending, which could weigh on higher-margin segments such as ice cream and other value-added products.
Overall, CIMB Securities said it has cut its FY26 to FY28 forecast core net profit for Farm Fresh by 2.7 to 6.2 per cent, mainly to reflect higher raw material costs and softer sales volumes.
Accordingly, the target price has been lowered to RM2.65 from RM3.00, based on 28 times the calendar year 2027 forecast price-to-earnings, in line with the stock's three-year historical mean.
Despite near-term headwinds, the firm believes the risks are largely priced in, noting the stock's 14.8 per cent decline over the past three months.
CIMB Securities has maintained a "Buy" call on Farm Fresh, underpinned by the group's leading position in Malaysia's dairy market, expanding regional footprint and diversified product portfolio.
The firm also noted that the stock is trading at an 11.4 per cent discount to its three-year historical average of 28 times, which it views as an attractive entry point.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DoE director-general pleads not guilty to RM100k bribe [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Department of Environment director-general has pleaded not guilty in the Sessions Court here today to accepting a RM100,000 bribe linked to an environmental approval process in Penang.
Datuk Wan Abdul Latiff Wan Jaffar, 57, is accused of corruptly receiving the cash from a company director through an intermediary as an inducement to consider an appeal for exemption from preparing an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) report for the construction of a dual carriageway at Bukit Kukus, Paya Terubong.
The alleged offence was committed at his office on Jan 15 last year.
He was charged under Section 16(a)(B) of the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) Act 2009, which carries a maximum penalty of 20 years' imprisonment and a fine of not less than five times the value of the bribe, or RM10,000, whichever is higher, upon conviction.
Earlier, deputy public prosecutor Datuk Ahmad Akram Gharib proposed bail at RM100,000, citing the accused's position, the amount involved, and the severity of the punishment if convicted. He also noted a risk of absconding.
Lawyer Mohd Firdaus Ahmad, representing the accused, requested a lower bail of RM20,000 to RM30,000, arguing the prosecution's proposal was excessive. He said his client, the sole breadwinner for his family, has three children still studying at public higher education institutions and a non-working wife.
"The purpose of bail is to ensure attendance in court at the stipulated time to answer the charge. The issue of absconding does not arise as my client has given full cooperation to the authorities. He was remanded on Jan 29, allowed seven days' remand, but released early on Feb 3 and returned to give his statement to the MACC on March 2. This shows his commitment and that he is not a flight risk," he said.
Judge Suzana Hussin set bail at RM40,000 with one surety and ordered the accused to surrender his passport to the court until the conclusion of the trial.
The court fixed May 7 for case management.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AG: Dashcam video, witness testimony justify murder charge in Klang DUI crash
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 3 — Citing clear evidence that a driver’s actions were “so imminently dangerous” that death was a near certainty, Attorney-General Tan Sri Mohd Dusuki Mokhtar today defended the decision to level a murder charge in the fatal Klang drink-driving crash.
Speaking on the charge against R. Saktygaanapathy, Dusuki explained that the case meets the threshold for murder under Section 300(d) of the Penal Code, the
New Straits Times
reported.
He noted the accused’s deliberate act of driving into the opposite lane at high speed, which created a highly perilous situation for the victim, delivery rider Amirul Hafiz Omar.
“The act of the accused entering the opposing lane intentionally and at high speed resulted in a situation that was ‘so imminently dangerous’ to the deceased,” the AG said.
Section 300(d) of the Penal Code defines murder as an act committed with the knowledge that it is so imminently dangerous it will likely cause death, and is carried out without any justification for taking such a risk.
The AG added that the prosecution’s case is supported by witness testimony and dashcam footage that captured the accused’s conduct at the time of the incident.
On April 1, Saktygaanapathy was charged at the Klang Magistrate’s Court with the murder of Amirul Hafiz, who was killed in a head-on collision along Jalan Raya Barat earlier that day.
Court documents revealed that Saktygaanapathy allegedly drove against traffic at high speed before the collision.
The impact flung the victim from his motorcycle onto another vehicle, and he was pronounced dead at the scene.
Saktygaanapathy also pleaded guilty to a separate charge of self-administering benzodiazepine and tetrahydrocannabinol (THC).

Title: Private GPs welcome revised RM10–RM80 consultation fee after 30-year freeze
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, April 3 — The Federation of Private Medical Practitioners’ Associations Malaysia (FPMPAM) has taken note of the gazettement of the revised general practitioner (GP) consultation fee schedule, which came into effect yesterday.
Its president, Dr Shanmuganathan T.V. Ganeson, said the revision marks the first in over 30 years and should be viewed as a long-overdue correction to an outdated fee structure, rather than a conventional fee increase.
“While the revised RM10 to RM80 range provides some flexibility, its real impact will depend on how it is implemented in practice, particularly within existing managed care and third-party administrator (TPA) arrangements, where pricing may not be fully market-driven.
“FPMPAM will be monitoring implementation closely to ensure that the intent of the revision is not undermined in practice,” he said in a statement today.
He noted that consultation fees under Schedule 13, which have remained unchanged since 2013, will require similar attention.
“As a federation representing both GPs and specialists, FPMPAM looks forward to further engagement with the authorities on this next phase,” he said.
Dr Shanmuganathan said the gazette should not be seen as an endpoint, but rather the beginning of continued reform to ensure a fair and sustainable primary care system.
“Reform must be continuous; otherwise, we risk repeating the cycle of delay and correction.
“FPMPAM also believes that the experience of this revision highlights the need for a more structured and regular mechanism for fee review, to avoid prolonged stagnation and ensure timely, evidence-based adjustments that balance affordability with the sustainability of medical practice,” he added.
The amendment, under P.U. (A) 150/2026 to Schedule 7 of the Private Healthcare Facilities and Services Regulations 2006, takes effect on April 2. — Bernama

Title: Selangor to launch special task force Monday to combat global energy crisis
Article:
SHAH ALAM, April 3 — The Selangor government will announce the establishment of a special task force by Monday at the latest, to address current challenges and their impact on the people’s lives following the global energy crisis.
Selangor Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said a meeting to finalise the establishment of the task force would be held today with members drawn from experts in various fields including economics, finance and industries such as oil and gas.
He said programme and activity proposals would be submitted by departments and government-linked companies (GLCs), before being scrutinised for implementation by the task force.
“This special task force is expected to focus on the aspects of people’s survival and the sustainability of the state’s economy, especially in facing potential risks related to food and energy supplies.
“The approach taken will also be coordinated with the federal government’s measures, including policies such as work from home, to ensure uniformity of implementation,” he said.
He said this at the Selangor State Level Jelajah Taat Setia MADANI programme here at Dewan Raja Muda Musa which was also attended by the Chief Secretary to the Government Tan Sri Shamsul Azri Abu Bakar.
Asked about Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim’s message to the Menteri Besar and Chief Ministers in a special meeting yesterday, Amirudin said it included ensuring basic supplies as well as risks that the country may face and emphasised the importance of cooperation between the state and federal governments.
Asked about the State Legislative Assembly Session scheduled for this April, he said it was taking place as usual because so far there was no need for extraordinary measures such as an emergency and administrative activities and state government programmes would continue as usual.
“The important thing now is to give explanation to the people about the steps that have been and will be taken,” he said. — Bernama

Title: JPJ eyes stricter DUI penalties, victim compensation after fatal crashes
Article:
PUTRAJAYA, April 3 — The Road Transport Department (JPJ) has now begun a review and revision of several sections under the Road Transport Act (APJ) 1987 [Act 333] to allow drivers under the influence of alcohol or drugs to be subject to heavier penalties.
JPJ director-general Datuk Aedy Fadly Ramli said JPJ, through its legal division, had begun a review and further study of two sections, namely Section 44 and Section 45 of APJ 1987.
He said the process was carried out first before it was brought to the attention of Transport Minister Anthony Loke and the Ministry of Transport (MOT) for any decision.
“The Minister of Transport has ordered the MOT and also the JPJ to review Section 44 and Section 45 under APJ.
“This is so that we can impose tougher and more decisive action on offenders involving drivers who are Driving Under the Influence (DUI), that is, drunk or drugged, so that we can impose heavier action,” he said.
Meeting reporters after a JPJ assembly here today, he said the JPJ would mobilise a more aggressive advocacy programme to raise awareness of the dangers of driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs.
“We will make preparations for more aggressive advocacy to combat drunk drivers on the road,” he said.
Regarding the proposal to establish a special court for major and serious accident cases, Aedy Fadly said the JPJ welcomed the proposal which could increase public awareness of road safety aspects.
“We welcome the decision to establish a separate court, but we understand that it will involve several implications, namely financial, manpower and so on.
“But if there is a government decision to establish a special court, we will very much welcome it,” he said.
Last Monday, Loke said his ministry was drafting further amendments to Section 44 of the Road Transport Act 1987 [Act 333], including mandating compensation to the victim’s family.
On March 29, a motorcyclist died in an accident on Jalan Raya Barat, Klang, while two days later another incident on Jalan Pintasan Kajang-Semenyih left six individuals including a child injured involved drunk drivers — Bernama

Title: Police hunt man filmed in viral sexual harassment incident at Kajang MRT
Article:
KAJANG, April 3 — Police are actively searching for a man captured in a viral video allegedly committing an act of sexual harassment at the Kajang MRT station.
Kajang district police chief ACP Naazron Abdul Yusof confirmed in a statement late Thursday that a 23-second video of the incident began circulating on the social media platform Threads at approximately 9.00am.
He said a scene investigation has been conducted at the location of the incident, which occurred at Gate A of the station.
“Through the viral recording, a man is believed to have committed the act. Efforts to track down and arrest the suspect are actively ongoing,” Naazron stated.
The case is being investigated under Section 509 of the Penal Code for insulting the modesty of a person. A conviction carries a penalty of up to five years in prison, a fine, or both.
Police are urging members of the public with any information about the suspect to come forward. They can contact the Kajang Police Headquarters at 03-89114222 or the investigating officer, Sergeant Nor Atiqah Anuar, at 013-7948922.
Naazron also advised the public not to speculate on the case or to further circulate the video, as it could cause public anxiety.
He warned that spreading the video could lead to action under Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998.

Title: Four-day remand for meth-positive trailer driver in JB-Segamat crash that killed van driver and married couple
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, April 3 — The trailer driver involved in a fatal accident involving three vehicles at Kilometre 212, Jalan Johor Bahru-Seremban near Taman Golden Hill intersection, Gemas Baharu, yesterday evening was remanded for four days starting today until next Monday.
Segamat district police chief, Supt. Mohd Jumazanzahir Chek Ismail when contacted said the remand order against the 28-year-old suspect was issued by Magistrate Amalina Johar at the Segamat Magistrate’s Court here, today.
Mohd Jumazanzahir said in the incident yesterday evening, the victims who died were a married couple and the van driver.
“The van driver was the brother of the wife of married couple while the two injured victims were the driver’s grandchildren,” he said briefly.
Earlier, the media reported that three individuals died while two others were slightly injured in an accident involving three vehicles at the Taman Golden Hill intersection, Gemas Baharu, yesterday evening.
Johor police chief Datuk CP Ab Rahaman Arsad said the incident at 3.45 pm was believed to have occurred when a trailer driven by a local man hit the back of a van carrying the victims.
“As a result of the collision, the van lost control before entering the opposite lane and colliding with a lorry,” he said in a statement early this morning.
He said the three victims in the van were confirmed dead at the scene due to serious injuries.
Ab Rahaman said two other van passengers suffered minor injuries and were taken to Segamat Hospital for further treatment, while the drivers of lorry and trailer were unhurt.
He said the trailer driver had been detained to assist in the investigation and initial urine screening results found the man to be positive for methamphetamine.
The case is being investigated under Section 44(1)(a) of the Road Transport Act 1987 for driving under the influence of drugs causing death, as well as Section 15(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952 for injecting drugs into the body. — Bernama

Title: Transit house caretaker and boat skipper among eight Indonesians under arrest for migrant smuggling in Pontian
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, April 3 — Police have arrested eight Indonesian men believed to be involved in migrant smuggling in the waters off Kukup near Pontian during Ops Pintas Bersepadu and Taring Landai on Wednesday and yesterday.
Pontian Police chief Supt Hadzrat Hussien Mion Hussain said the suspects, aged between 21 and 36, were picked up during the joint operation between the district police’s Criminal Investigation Division and the Kukup Region Two Marine Police.
He said that during a Wednesday operation at 8 pm, five individuals were arrested, comprising a suspected caretaker of a transit house, a boat skipper, and three migrants.
“Follow-up arrests yesterday at about 6 pm led to the detention of three more Indonesian men believed to be acting as a transporter, boat skipper and an assistant caretaker of the transit house,” he said in a statement today.
He said the operation was the result of two weeks of intelligence gathering and surveillance focusing on areas with potential for such activities, including fish cage farms in the waters off Kukup.
